A comprehensive review on microbial urease: features and industrial applications

Amiya Ojhaa Department of Bioengineering, NIT Agartala, Agartala, Tripura, India
,
Tarun Kanti Bandyopadhyayb Department of Chemical Engineering, NIT Agartala, Agartala, India
&
Deeplina Dasa Department of Bioengineering, NIT Agartala, Agartala, Tripura, IndiaCorrespondencedeeplina.bio@nita.ac.in deeplina06@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-7889-3780
Pages 1-24 | Received 09 Jun 2023, Accepted 19 May 2025, Published online: 12 Nov 2025
  • Cite this article
  • https://doi.org/10.1080/07388551.2025.2529589IF: 7.7 Q1 B2
  • CrossMark

?Mathematical formulae have been encoded as MathML and are displayed in this HTML version using MathJax in order to improve their display. Uncheck the box to turn MathJax off. This feature requires Javascript. Click on a formula to zoom.

Abstract

Urease (urea amidohydrolase, EC 3.5.1.5), first crystallized from jack-bean (Canavalia ensiformis) by James B. Sumner in 1926, has become a cornerstone of biotechnology. The global urease market, dominated by plant-based sources, was valued at USD 1.24 Billion in 2024 and is projected to grow at a CAGR of 5.5%, reaching USD 1.94 billion by 2033. However, plant-derived ureases face challenges, such as low extraction efficiency, variability in yield due to plant maturity, and sensitivity to environmental factors, limiting scalability. Microbial ureases, globally embraced due to escalating demand, offer superior stability across extreme pH and temperature ranges. These attributes confer broad potential applications in diverse fields, such as: agriculture, environmental, clinical, and healthcare industries. Nevertheless, the industrial production of microbial urease continues to encounter obstacles, including elevated purification costs and the lack of cost-effective optimization strategies. This review provides quantitative insights into microbial ureases from bacteria, fungi (excluding hemiascomyces), and diatoms, highlighting their catalytic efficiency, Ni-dependencies, and advancements in assay techniques and enhanced purification strategies. It explores applications across agriculture, bioremediation, and self-healing concrete, emphasizing ureolysis-driven microbially induced carbonate precipitation (MICP) as a promising eco-friendly and sustainable approach, thus providing a scientific and reasonable reference for their large-scale application.

GRAPHICAL ABSTRACT

Keywords:

  • Microbial urease
  • MICP
  • urease activity
  • anti-cancer activity
  • self-healing concrete
  • urea

Introduction

The worldwide demand for enzymes was estimated to be at USD 9.84 billion in 2023 and is expected to grow at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 8.7% from 2024 to 2035 [Citation1]. As the enzyme market continues to expand, the global urease market is also experiencing a comprehensive growth trend, driven by increased demand from the agricultural and industrial sectors, and is expected to register a CAGR of 5.5% within the forecast period of 2022–2030 [Citation2]. The pursuit of novel enzymes with industrial applications has prompted researchers to focus on enzyme engineering, as naturally occurring enzymes are often deemed unsuitable due to their poor yield and limited stability [Citation3]. Currently, more than 75% of industrial enzymes are members of the hydrolase family and are employed for the degradation of diverse natural substrates [Citation4]. Urease is a ubiquitous cytosolic enzyme belonging to the hydrolase family that cleaves linear amide (C-N) bonds and is hence referred to as an amidohydrolase enzyme [E.C. 3.5.1.5]. Urease, found universally in plants, bacteria, several fungi, and algae, acts upon its natural substrate, urea, breaking it down into ammonia (NH3) and carbamate (CH2NO2−). The carbamate subsequently undergoes hydrolysis into carbonic acid (H2CO3) and produces an additional molecule of ammonia, thereby raising the environmental pH [Citation5,Citation6]. This rise in pH is exploited by many bacteria and fungi to infect the host and exert their virulence [Citation7]. Ureases obligatorily require nickel for their catalytic activity or as a cofactor, and hence are also termed mettaloenzymes and phosphotriesterases, which display catalytically active metal in their active sites [Citation8]. The prominence of nickel in urease activity was revealed by Dixon et al. [Citation9]. The metal preference or promiscuity of a metalloenzyme is set by the combination of different factors, such as hard/soft interactions, the Irving-Williams series, and the quantity of the metal in the surroundings and inside the cell [Citation10,Citation11]. In 1864, van Tiehem achieved the groundbreaking isolation of the first ureolytic bacterium, Micrococcus ureae, while in 1890, Miquel introduced the term “urease” [Citation12]. Since their discovery, ureases have evolved into indispensable biocatalysts, playing a pivotal role in diverse industries, such as environmental management, agriculture, clinical care, and diagnostics [Citation13,Citation14]. Additionally, urease enzymes, are gaining importance in the treatment of human, animal, and crop diseases [Citation15,Citation16].

Despite these advances, developing an extraction method with higher urease efficiency, a simpler procedure and reduced time and labor costs remains essential for future large-scale production. While plant derived ureases, particularly from jack bean and soybean, dominate the commercial market, several factors, such as cell wall composition, plant age, temperature, and pH, significantly influence extraction efficiency [Citation17,Citation18]. Nevertheless, microbial sources present a more promising alternative due to their higher adaptability, stability in complex environments, and faster growth, which results in higher urease yields [Citation19,Citation20]. Interestingly, ureolysis-driven microbial carbonate precipitation has emerged as a rapidly growing research area, attracting considerable attention due to its feasibility and potential applications across various environmental and geotechnical sectors [Citation21], aligning with global priorities outlined within the paradigm of the Sustainable Development Goals, as illustrated in the graphical abstract. Therefore, to harness the full potential of microbial ureases, further research is required to optimize production methods, enhance extraction efficiency, and assess their scalability for commercial applications.

This review delves into mechanistic insights into Ni-transfer and the regulatory mechanisms governing urease maturation, along with an in-depth analysis of the catalytic mechanism of ureolysis in ureolytic microbes. It also covers the advantages and limitations of prevalent urease assay techniques, state-of-the-art purification strategies, and the operational parameters of microbial urease, including pH, temperature, and yields. Additionally, the paper retraces the applications of microbial ureases in agricultural, environmental, therapeutic, and clinical sectors. Lastly, the current glitches and future opportunities of microbial ureases are discussed.

Amidohydrolase and amidolyase enzyme

Urea is catabolized primarily by two hydrolase enzymes, urease (EC 3.5.1.5) and urea amidolyase (EC 3.5.1.45) as illustrated in . Urease is prevalent in bacteria, fungi (except for hemiascomyces), diatoms, and plants, but not in green algae and animals. Urea +H2O→NH2COOH+NH3

Figure 1. Catabolism of urea occurs through various microbial pathways, with two major enzymes playing crucial roles: urease or urea amidohydrolase (EC 3.5.1.5) and amidolyase (EC 3.5.1.45).

In contrast, the hydrolysis of urea by urea amidolyase is dependent on ATP, NADP, or biotin as a coenzyme. Urea amidolyase composed of two catalytic motifs, urea carboxylase (converts urea to urea 1-craboxylate or allophanate) and allophanate hydrolase (degrade allophanate to NH3 and CO2) [Citation22]. Urea carboxylase is a bifunctional enzyme that catalyzes the sequential processes of urea caboxylation and allophanate production by two distinct catalytic domains, a biotin carboxylase domain and a carboxyltransferase domain [Citation23]. urea + ATP + HCO3→urea−1−carboxylate + ADP + Piurea−1−carboxylate + H2O → 2NH3+ 2CO2

Various metabolic pathways cause urea to accumulate in the microbial cells. The enzyme arginase is the prime enzyme of the urea cycle in: bacteria, yeasts, plants, invertebrates, and vertebrates, and catalyzes the conversion of L-arginine to L-ornithine and urea [Citation24]. The conversion of allantoate to glyoxylate, an intermediate of microbial purine metabolism, is carried out by a two-step mechanism. Allantoate is initially degraded by allantoicase (allantoate amidinohydrolase) into urea and ureidoglycolate [Citation25]. The latter substance is again converted to urea and glyoxylate by ureidoglycolase (ureidoglycolate amidinohydrolase) [Citation26]. Microbial degradation of atrazine (2-chloro-4-ethylamino-6-isopropylamino-s-triazine), a well-known herbicide, can lead to increased urea accumulation in soil. Five genera belonging to: Bacillus, Enterobacter, Pseudomonas, Ochrobactrum, and Providencia, can metabolize atrazine in contaminated soils [Citation27,Citation28]. In atrazine degradation, cyanamide is hydrolyzed by cyanamide hydrolase to release urea, which is then converted into carbon dioxide and ammonia with the help of the urease enzyme.

Diverse architectures of microbial ureases

The first crystal structure of microbial urease from Klebsiella aerogenes was unraveled in 1995 by X-ray crystallography (resolution 2.2 Å) [Citation29], followed by urease Sporosarcina pasteurii (formerly known as Bacillus pasteurii, resolution 1.65 Å) and, since then, several other structures of the enzyme, with or without bound inhibitors have been determined [Citation30]. As per the data retrieved from the Expasy enzyme database, to date, 827 crystal structures of urease proteins have been deposited in Uniprot and PDB (https://enzyme.expasy.org/cgi-bin/enzyme/get-enzyme-entry?3.5.1.5). The urease structural genes in K. aerogenes and S. pasteurii consist of ureA, ureB, and ureC, encoding the γ, β, and α subunits, respectively [Citation31], as shown in . This gene configuration is typical among most characterized bacterial ureases. However, Helicobacter pylori stands out as an exception, possessing only ureA and ureB genes due to a gene fusion event [Citation32]. Notably, the ureA gene encodes both α and β subunits within a single polypeptide chain, presenting a unique structural arrangement compared to other bacterial ureases (). Urease monomers in plants like Canavalia ensiformis have a large molecular weight because they are formed by the joining of three bacterial α, β, and γ subunits [Citation33]. In all instances, ureases exhibit similar secondary and tertiary structures: α subunits possess a TIM barrel and a β-sheet domain, β subunits are primarily composed of β-sheets on the outer surface, and γ subunits contain domains comprising both α-helices and β-sheets [Citation34]. While the quaternary structure of bacterial ureases varies, exhibiting different chain topologies and higher-order oligomeric assemblies, certain patterns emerge (). Ureases from K. aerogenes and S. pasteurii assemble into heterotrimers composed of ureA, ureB, and ureC proteins, which further oligomerize into trimers ([αβγ]3) [Citation16]. Conversely, Yersinia enterocolitica shares the same structural gene configuration but forms a dodecameric hollow sphere with a ([αβγ]3]4) oligomeric structure of tetrahedral symmetry, as observed in its crystal structure [Citation35] (). In the case of H. pylori urease, four (αβ)3 trimers combine to form a spherically shaped tetramer of trimers [(αβ)3]4), containing twelve active sites [Citation36,Citation37]. To date, the assembled crystal structure of fungal ureases remains unreported, but fungal urease is confirmed to adopt a homohexameric structure ([α3]2), similar to the plant ureases from C. ensiformis [Citation38].

Figure 2. (a) Schematic representation of diverse architecture of microbial ureases functional genes and structural symmetry of K. aerogenes, H. pylori, and Y. enterocolitica. (b) Role of accessory proteins (Metallochaperones) in microbial urease maturation. (c) The crystal structure of (UreDFG)2 in H. pylori (PDB ID: 4HI0) reveals UreD (analogous to UreH) depicted in cyan blue, UreF in green, UreG in orange, and the metal ions binding site marked in black. The dimer interface of the Ni(II) binding motif is accentuated in the yellow-colored imidazole rings of Histidine residues. (d) Sequential reactions in the active site converting urea to ammonia and carbamate.

The role of accessory proteins in microbial urease maturation and Ni-transfer

In microbial cells, urease begins as an inactive apo-enzyme, and its activation entails a post-translational process involving four accessory proteins: UreD (or UreH in H. pylori, analogous to UreD in K. aerogenes), UreF, UreG, and UreE [Citation16,Citation39]. In the genomes of most ureolytic organisms, structural and accessory proteins are encoded by genes clustered in the same or different operons. While a complex formation between urease apoprotein (apoU) and accessory proteins is proposed, the precise mechanism of protein binding and their specific functions remain unclear. Urease activation necessitates the formation and dissociation of protein complexes with various combinations of the four accessory proteins, regulated by Ni(II) ions, GTP hydrolysis, and CO2 uptake for lysine carbamylation as shown in .

In vivo, the first interaction with the apoenzyme occurs between UreD and serves as a protein scaffold, facilitating subsequent interactions with other accessory proteins. Once the urease-UreD complex forms, UreF binds to it, inducing a conformational rearrangement of the enzyme through interaction with UreD. UreF then serves as the binding site for UreG, forming a supercomplex (UreDFG) that pre-activates apo-urease before Ni(II) incorporation, a critical modification for the enzyme to develop a functional active site [Citation40]. UreG, an intrinsically disordered GTPase, energizes the urease maturation process by dimerization, bringing the conserved Cys-Pro-His (CPH) motif to the dimer interface to bind a Ni(II) ion in a square planar coordination [Citation41,Citation42]. Notably, the crystal structure of (UreDFG)2 in H. pylori has been reported and is depicted in .

Additionally, in H. pylori, the accessory chaperone HypA for [Ni,Fe]-hydrogenase plays a role in transferring Ni(II) ions to the urease system by interacting with UreE2 in a Ni-UreE2-HypA assembly [Citation43]. According to the classical hypothesis, UreE serves as a metallochaperone that delivers Ni(II) ions to UreG via protein-protein interactions, forming a stable and active (UreE)2(UreG)2 complex in the presence of Mg2+ and GTP, although the exact mechanism remains largely unknown. In vivo, UreE exists as a homodimer in solution containing the conserved Gly-Asn-Arg-His motif that binds a Ni(II) ion at the dimeric interface [Citation44,Citation45]. UreG thus transitions between the UreDFG and UreEG complexes, depending on the nucleotide and metal ion-bound states [Citation46]. Upon nickel binding, UreG forms a stable dimer in a GTP-dependent manner and further interacts with the UreFD dimer and apo-urease to facilitate lysine carbamylation [Citation16,Citation39]. The carbamylation process occurs upon GTP hydrolysis by UreG, potentially through the formation of the carbamylating agent carboxyphosphate in the presence of CO2.

Catalytic mechanism of microbial urease and its nickel dependency

Urease’s substrate versatility extends beyond urea to include various analogues like formamide, acetamide, and others, along with different phosphoric acid amides and esters [Citation34]. A notable structural element of ureases is the mobile helix-turn-helix motif (Flap) within the α-subunit, which envelops the active site cavity. This flap, found in open or closed conformations, plays a crucial role in regulating substrate entry and product exit during catalysis. Urease activity relies on a binuclear active site comprising two Ni(II) ions, connected by a carbamylated lysine’s carboxylate group, essential for maintaining the optimal distance between ions during catalysis. Additionally, a hydroxide ion acts as the nucleophile in the hydrolysis reaction. Common and effective nickel binding sites in amino acid side chains include the imidazole nitrogen of His and the thiol sulfur of Cys residues. Microbial structures consistently exhibit a dinuclear active site with Ni(II) ions positioned 3.5–3.7 Å apart, bridged by oxygen atoms from a carbamylated lysine and bound to two histidines [Citation34,Citation47]. As mentioned earlier, nickel incorporation into urease’s active site occurs during enzyme maturation, with assistance from four accessory proteins: UreD, UreF, UreG, and UreE.

A number of putative hypotheses have been made regarding the catalytic mechanism of urease; however, the most prevalent one is S. pasteurii. The two nickel ions (Ni1 and Ni2) ions in S. pasteurii urease’s active site are bridged by a carbamylated lysine and an oxygen donor. Additionally, in addition to the bridges, one of the nickel ions (Ni1) is coordinated by two histidines (αHis 246 and αHis272) and a water molecule (W1). The coordination of Ni2 is similar to that of Ni1 and includes two histidine residues (αHis 136 and αHis134), a terminally bound aspartate (αAsp360), and a water molecule (W2). Together with W1 and W2, another terminal water molecule (W3), and a hydroxide ion (OH) in a nickel-bridging position (WB) form an H-bonded water tetrahedral cluster in the active site [Citation48]. The existence of this pseudo-tetrahedral arrangement of closed-spaced solvent molecules (O–-O interatomic distances in the 2.1–2.4 range) around the di-nickel cluster, implying the presence of a four-centered hydrogen-bonding network involving a proton located in the center of the tetrahedron formed by W1, W2, W3, and WB. The enzyme’s active site comprises a 30-residue super-secondary motif (helix-turn helix) with conserved residues αCys319 and αHis320 [Citation5]. This conformation renders the active site highly mobile (referred to as the mobile flap), making it accessible to urea, which initially binds to Ni1 through its carbonyl oxygen atom. Subsequently, nitrogen atoms of urea facilitate binding with Ni (II), with αAla167, αGly277, and αAla363 serving as H-bond acceptors for the formation of the bidentate bond. In the bidentate nickel-binding mode of urea, both Ni (II) ions play a direct role in substrate binding and activation. This bond is believed to facilitate the water nucleophilic attack on the carbonyl carbon, resulting in a tetrahedral intermediate from which NH3 and carbamate are released. Several hypotheses have been made regarding this nucleophilic attack show in . Based on the structure of an inhibitor complex, Benini et al. suggested a mechanism involving a secondary coordination of one of the urea nitrogen atoms to Ni(II), which positions the substrate for an attack from the bridging hydroxide (bridging hydroxide hypothesis) [Citation49]. Pearson et al., have suggested that the nucleophile is not the bridging hydroxide but rather a water/hydroxide coordinated to Ni(II) [Citation50]. The bridging hydroxide was further supported after the publication of kinetic data on fluoride inhibition of the urease enzyme from K. aerogenes [Citation51]. The crystal structure of urease bound to boric acid from S. pasteurii provided additional confirmation about the bridging hydroxide mechanism, where B(OH)3 acts as a substrate analog [Citation52]. Carlsson and Nordlander revealed that the formation of a tetrahedral intermediate based on the bridging hydroxyl group is energetically unfavorable, suggesting nucleophilic attack by a terminally bound hydroxide [Citation53]. In 2019, Mazzei et al. have strengthened the bridging hydroxide postulates and the formation of bidentate bonds through the fluoride ion inhibition model of urease [Citation54]. Nonetheless, a urease variant with a similar structure but exhibiting low catalytic efficiency has been observed in Helicobacter mustelae. This variant is distinguished by the absence of Ni(II) ions in its active site, replaced instead by Fe(II) ions [Citation34].

Assessing urease activity: common assay methods

The most common and straightforward method for quantifying urease activity is conductivity measurement, as it directly reflects the breakdown of urea by ureolytic microbes (as depicted in ). Conductivity meters are used to detect changes in electrical conductivity following the introduction of bacterial cultures into a urea solution. The enzyme urease catalyzes the hydrolysis of urea, a nonionic molecule, into ammonium and bicarbonate ions, leading to an increase in the medium’s conductivity. The urease-producing strain was cultured in nutrient broth (NB) medium and allowed to incubate for 96 h at 30 °C with a 120-rpm rotating shaker. During the incubation, 1 ml of bacterial suspension was periodically withdrawn from the medium and added to a test tube containing 9 ml of 1.11 M urea, followed by 5-min incubation at 30 ± 2 °C. The increase in conductivity of the reaction mixture over time is directly correlated with the absorbance at 600 nm [Citation20,Citation55,Citation56].

Figure 3. Estimation of urease activity, (a) 96-well plate-based assay; (b) Berthelot’s reagent or phenol-hypochlorite based assay; (c) Nessler’s reagent-based assay; (d) Conductivity based assay.

Another common method of detecting released ammonia after the hydrolysis of urea is the Nessler assay, which produces yellow/brown complex as mentioned in equation [Citation18,Citation57]. The enzymatic reaction was stopped using H2SO4 (1 mol/l) and the absorbance was monitored at 480 nm. The absorption of yellow/brown complex is directly related to the amount of liberated ammonia from the substrate urea. NH3+ H2O + 2K2HgI4+ 3KOH = HgO.HgNH2I + 7KI + 3H2O

Using Berthelot’s reagent or a modified phenol-hypochlorite assay is another colorimetric approach for evaluating urease activity from the amount of released ammonia from the reaction mixture [Citation18] as shown in . The conversion of sodium nitropruside to indophenol in an alkaline environment by the released ammonia from the culture supernatant was used to determine the strain’s urease activity. The absorbance of blue colored complex was measured at 640–660 nm [Citation58]. The accuracy of this method is pH dependent and less applicable to cyanobacteria or plants as they produce interfering molecules like chlorophyll and pigments that has same absorbance maxima [Citation59].

The rapid detection of urea-degrading microbes in samples is facilitated by Christensen’s urea agar medium. Within this medium, microbes utilize urea as their primary nitrogen source, generating ammonia that elevates the pH above 8.0, leading to a distinct color shift from yellow to vibrant fuchsia pink. Recently, these principles have been adapted for quantifying and screening microbial urease activity in 96-well plates (as shown in ), aided by hydrochloric acid-soaked cotton to prevent ammonia evaporation. The intensity of the color change, indicative of urease activity, was quantified by measuring absorbance at 560 nm [Citation60–62]. Similarly, the microplate-based rapid urease tests (RUT), used to evaluate urease activity in oral bacteria, and utilizes a 2% urea solution in peptone broth with phenol red at pH 6.0. This pH indicator transitions from yellow under acidic conditions to pink, red, and ultimately purple under alkaline conditions, indicating urease activity [Citation63]. While these modified semi-quantitative assays have limitations, such as their restricted timeframe for accurately detecting urease activity and the potential for false positives due to bacterial byproducts or unrelated pH changes, incorporating controls without bacteria, urea, and urease-producing strains is crucial for overcoming these challenges [Citation64]. However, several alternative techniques have been effectively employed for the detection of urease production and activity, such as the pH increment method [Citation57], titration method [Citation65,Citation66]. The one unit of urease activity is defined as the amount of enzyme required to release 1 µmoL of ammonia from urea per minute at optimum pH and temperature. Enzyme Activity of urease (U/mL)=A480×C×VM×t×v

A480= Optical Density (OD) at 480 nm (in case of Nessler assay); C = 1 OD equivalent of NH4Cl (mg/mL) from standard plot; V = Volume of the reaction mixture (mL); t = Reaction Time (min); M = Molar mass of NH4Cl (53.49 g/mol); v = Enzyme source (mL). Specific activity of urease U/mg =Total enzyme activityU/mLTotal proteinmg/mL

Urease was the first enzyme precipitated as crystal protein from jack bean (Canavalia ensiformis), signifying the proteinaceous nature of the enzyme [Citation67]. This landmark discovery also unveiled the presence of sulfhydryl groups within proteins, demonstrated for the first time in urease. The enzymatic 7breakdown of urea into ammonia and carbamic acid by urease operates independently of ATP and NADP but relies on nickel as a cofactor. While plant-based ureases were initially identified, microbial ureases have garnered more extensive exploration. documents a comprehensive screening of ureases across various microbial sources, detailing their maximal urease activity and potential applications.

Table 1. Various microbial sources for urease production, including isolation medium, activity, and industrial applications.

Download CSVDisplay Table

Purification strategies, optimal parameters, and yields of microbial ureases

Purification of microbial urease is a critical area of research aimed at enhancing its efficiency, stability, and specificity. Urease, a large multimeric enzyme (commonly hexameric with multiple subunits), poses considerable challenges in purification due to its size and sensitivity to environmental changes. It is highly susceptible to heat, pH fluctuations, and chemical agents, which may cause denaturation or loss of activity during purification. Moreover, urease depends on the presence of nickel ions (Ni2+) for its catalytic activity, and the loss of nickel during purification often leads to enzyme inactivation, making reactivation difficult. The optimal activity of microbial urease typically occurs between 37 and 50 °C and in an alkaline pH range, attributed to ammonia release during the hydrolysis of urea [Citation102–104]. Kinetic studies reveal that microbial urease generally exhibits a Kₘ value between 1 and 5 mM, indicating a high affinity for its substrate [Citation102,Citation105,Citation106]. However, the purification process is further complicated by the presence of various inhibitors, including elemental ions (e.g., Hg2+ and Ag+), inorganic ions (e.g., boric acid), and organic compounds (e.g., acetohydroxamic acid and hydroquinone), all of which can adversely affect urease activity [Citation107,Citation108].

Historically, urease purification methodologies have transitioned from basic approaches to more refined and efficient techniques. Early methods relied on simple precipitation, solvent extraction, and fractionation, often yielding impure preparations with limited activity and stability. Modern purification strategies, however, have significantly improved, incorporating sophisticated chromatographic techniques, such as: ion exchange, size exclusion, affinity chromatography, and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), which offer enhanced purity, yield, and enzyme stability [Citation109–112]. Various researchers have employed different approaches to extract urease from jack bean and other plant sources [Citation18,Citation113]. However, the extraction of microbial urease remains an expensive and complex process, resulting in significantly less literature being available in this research area [Citation83,Citation105,Citation114]. provides a generalized overview of microbial urease purification strategies based on the existing literature. Microbial urease is extracted either intracellularly or extracellularly, followed by precipitation using agents like ammonium sulfate or acetone [Citation83,Citation115]. The resulting precipitate is dissolved in an alkaline buffer and centrifuged, and the purified fractions obtained from chromatography are subjected to sodium dodecyl sulfate-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) to determine the molecular weight of urease. The results from SDS-PAGE are further validated using zymography or assessed for urease activity through substrate-dependent ammonia release assays. A comprehensive analysis of the existing literature highlights various urease extraction and purification techniques, along with kinetic parameters, yield, and molecular weight across bacteria, fungi, and cyanobacteria, as summarized in . Despite advancements, microbial urease purification remains an intricate process requiring further exploration and optimization to meet industrial and biomedical demands.

Figure 4. Schematic representation of microbial urease purification strategies, molecular mass determination, zymogram analysis, and assessment of kinetic parameters.

Table 2. Microbial urease, along with purification strategies, optimized process conditions, kinetic parameters, molecular mass, yield percentage, and fold purification.

Download CSVDisplay Table

Prospective industrial applications of ureolytic microbes

Urease exhibits remarkable versatility across multiple industries, finding extensive applications in environmental, medical, food, and agricultural sectors. In wastewater treatment, it facilitates urea degradation, while in the medical field, it plays a critical role in biosensors for urea detection [Citation125], drug delivery systems [Citation126], and the investigation of Helicobacter pylori infections [Citation36]. In the food industry, urease enhances the flavor of fermented products, and in agriculture, urease inhibitors optimize the efficiency of urea-based fertilizers [Citation127]. Notably, urease contributes to bioremediation processes for heavy metal removal and supports biocementation through MICP, a process driven by ureolysis that stabilizes soil. MICP has gained considerable attention in geotechnical engineering due to its controllable conditions and impressive efficiency, enabling the precipitation of up to 90% calcium carbonate within a relatively short period [Citation128]. Ongoing research continues to expand the potential applications of MICP, including: soil enhancement [Citation21,Citation71,Citation76], pollutant containment [Citation80–82,Citation84], mitigation of seismic liquefaction, enhanced oil recovery, and the restoration of fractures in stone and concrete structures. This growing interest is evidenced by a significant body of research, with approximately 1384 articles published in the past five years (from 2021 to March 2025), as recorded in the Web of Science database. The reaction cascade of CaCO3 formation by ureolytic microbes is depicted in , highlighting the intricate biochemical processes involved.

Figure 5. Overview of urea hydrolysis by urease in microbial cells and subsequent precipitation of CaCO₃ with their potential role in enhancing soil fertility, bioremediation of heavy metal-contaminated sites, and self-healing concrete.

Bioremediation

Bioremediation through MICP via ureolysis has gained prominence as a sustainable and effective approach for the immobilization of heavy metals and environmental detoxification. Ureolytic microbes hydrolyze urea to produce ammonia and carbonate ions, leading to a localized pH increase that facilitates the precipitation of metal ions as stable carbonates. This mechanism effectively reduces the bioavailability and mobility of hazardous metals, preventing their leaching into the environment. For instance, a newly discovered Cd-tolerant ureolytic bacterium, DL-1, demonstrated a remarkable Cd2+ removal efficiency of 99.89% through MICP, highlighting its potential for cadmium remediation [Citation129]. Similarly, high-throughput sequencing revealed that MICP-treated sludge enriched with ureolytic Sporosarcina sp. reduced the soluble exchangeable Pb fraction by 100% and the Cd fraction by 48.54%, showcasing the efficacy of MICP in heavy metal immobilization [Citation130]. Further studies demonstrated that Terrabacter tumescens successfully immobilized a range of heavy metals, including: Cd, Co, Ni, Pb, Cu, and Zn, precipitating them as their respective carbonates (CdCO3, CoCO3, NiCO3, PbCO3, CuCO3, and ZnCO3), thus minimizing their environmental impact [Citation131]. In addition to bacterial applications, marine fungal strain Rhodotorula sp. MZ312369 demonstrated remarkable efficiency by eliminating 50 ppm and 400 ppm of Zn2+ and Cr6+, respectively, within 7 days of incubation using the promising CaCO3 bioprecipitation technique [Citation132]. Similarly, Dhami et al. reported that two ureolytic isolates, Aspergillus sp. UF3 and Fusarium oxysporum UF8, exhibited significant calcite production along with co-precipitation of heavy metals such as Pb and radionuclide Sr as carbonates, in synergism with carbonic anhydrase [Citation133]. Beyond bacteria and fungi, the cyanobacterium Gloeomargarita lithophora demonstrated exceptional potential by removing nearly 99% of radioactive elements, including 90Strontium and 226Radium, from contaminated environments, thereby expanding the scope of MICP beyond heavy metals [Citation134].

The precipitation of heavy metals via MICP is also influenced by microbial growth, making it essential to optimize factors, such as temperature, pH, and nutrient availability, before successful implementation. Most urease-producing microorganisms exhibit optimal heavy metal fixation at temperatures between 25 and 35 °C and a pH range of approximately 7–8 [Citation135]. A study conducted by Qiao et al. highlighted the varying toxicity of heavy metals to MICP bacteria, ranking them in the following order: Cd > Zn > Ni > Cu. Interestingly, the study also confirmed that urease-producing bacteria could co-precipitate multiple heavy metals even without inherent tolerance to them, reinforcing MICP as an effective biological approach worth further investigation for ecological restoration [Citation136]. To optimize MICP for bioremediation, two primary strategies are employed: biostimulation and bioaugmentation. Biostimulation involves modifying the environment to activate native urease-producing bacteria, while bioaugmentation introduces exogenous microbes to enhance the precipitation of target contaminants. According to Ghasemi et al., biostimulation is a critical pretreatment step that can, in some cases, replace bioaugmentation in soil biocalcification processes [Citation137]. Despite its high efficiency and eco-friendly approach, MICP-based bioremediation faces challenges such as ammonia byproduct generation and the need for precise environmental control to maintain microbial activity.

Self-healing concrete

In the current era of global urbanization, researchers are exploring alternative construction materials with a lower carbon footprint to address environmental concerns. Traditional concrete remediation is not only time-consuming but also environmentally damaging. Moreover, the safe disposal of construction waste remains a pragmatic challenge. One promising solution is self-healing techniques, particularly biomineralization-based self-healing, where microbes initiate CaCO3 precipitation to facilitate the autogenous healing of cracks in concrete. Ureolytic microbes produce various polymorphs of CaCO3, among which calcite is the most thermodynamically stable and predominant form, making it highly suitable for applications in self-healing concrete and bio-cementation. This process naturally occurs over time, extending the lifespan of structures and reducing maintenance expenses. Additionally, this method leverages the innate self-repair capabilities of concrete while addressing environmental concerns related to CO2 emissions from the cement industry, contributing to the fulfillment of SDG 9. The formation of CaCO3 precipitates within the concrete matrix aids in crack healing and enhances matrix properties.

Several experimental investigations have been conducted to identify the most suitable microbes for enhancing concrete performance under different environmental conditions, considering factors such as varying temperatures and high alkaline pH. These studies revealed that bacterial species, such as: Sporosarcina pasteurii, Bacillus subtilis, Bacillus megaterium, Bacillus sphaericus, and Bacillus cereus, are capable of precipitating CaCO3 via ureolysis, facilitating the healing of cracks of varying widths, as shown in . Specifically, Bacillus cereus can effectively seal mortar cracks ranging from 162 to 670 µm [Citation161], while Bacillus megaterium significantly reduces concrete permeability and enhances its strength [Citation162]. Similarly, Bacillus subtilis demonstrated improvements of 22% in compressive strength, 16% in split tensile strength, and 11% in flexural strength compared to conventional concrete after 28 days of curing [Citation163]. Recently, two new bacterial strains, B. flexus and S. koreensis, isolated from cementitious soil, exhibited remarkable increases in compressive strength of 21.8% and 12.2%, respectively, at 7 days, along with crack closure in widths of 259.7 and 288.7 µm [Citation164].

Table 3. Substantial crack healing accomplished by ureolytic microbes through calcium carbonate precipitation.

Download CSVDisplay Table

In comparison with bacterial strains, ureolytic fungi have been comparatively less explored for self-healing concrete preparation due to a lack of precise understanding of the fungal mechanisms involved in calcite production, a stable polymorph essential for crack healing [Citation158,Citation165]. However, unlike bacteria, fungi form large, extensive mycelial networks and possess durable cell walls due to the presence of chitin, which enhances their ability to survive in harsh concrete environments [Citation156,Citation158]. The efficacy of crack sealing, with widths of up to 1.3 mm, has been demonstrated using selected fungal species, such as Rhizopus oryzae, Fusarium oxysporum, and Trichoderma longibrachiatum, which also exhibited improvements in compressive and tensile strength, similar to bacterial strains [Citation156,Citation157]. All these fungal strains have confirmed their potential for calcite production as observed by XRD, FESEM, and TGA analysis, suggesting that they could play a significant role in the development of bio-cement in future research.

The efficiency of MICP for self-healing concrete generation is governed by several physicochemical and biological factors, including: the nature of microorganisms [Citation68], the pH and temperature of the environment [Citation166,Citation167], the concentration of calcium ions [Citation168,Citation169], the availability of dissolved inorganic carbon [Citation69,Citation75], and the presence of nucleation sites [Citation56]. The alkaline environment of concrete (pH ∼12–13) presents challenges to microbial viability, while temperature plays a pivotal role in regulating urease activity and carbonate precipitation, both of which are critical for MICP efficiency. Urease activity remains relatively stable at 35 °C but declines by 47% at 55 °C [Citation170,Citation171]. Although higher temperatures (around 50 °C) enhance carbonate precipitation, leading to a threefold increase compared to 25 °C, the resulting crystals are relatively small (2–5 μm in diameter), compromising mechanical strength and durability. Conversely, at lower temperatures (∼25 °C), CaCO3 crystals exhibit larger sizes (15–20 μm in diameter) and are predominantly deposited within the soil pore body, contributing to improved durability and mechanical properties [Citation172]. Despite higher precipitation rates at elevated temperatures, bio-cemented samples treated at 50 °C exhibit a 60% reduction in strength compared to those treated at 25 °C, highlighting that lower temperatures favor the formation of more stable and mechanically robust carbonate structures. Effective bacterial fixation within the concrete matrix is also essential for uniform CaCO3 precipitation and enhanced mechanical properties. Studies have shown that introducing high-salinity fixation solutions enhances bacterial adsorption, resulting in a more homogeneous distribution of bacteria and calcium carbonate, ultimately improving compressive strength [Citation173–175]. While MICP-based self-healing systems show immense promise, challenges such as ammonia buildup, maintaining microbial viability under extreme conditions, and achieving scalability in practical applications remain significant hurdles that require further investigation and optimization.

Ureolytic microbes in agricultural sector

Ureolytic microbes play a crucial role in soil nitrogen cycling by converting urea, a common nitrogen fertilizer, into ammonium, a form readily taken up by plants. This process enhances nitrogen availability for plants, essential for their growth and development [Citation127]. There are primarily two types of microbial ureases present in soil: extracellular, which regulates the breakdown of urea in soil, and intracellular, which is kept inside the microbial cell. Intracellular urease accounts for 37.1% to 73.1% of total urease and has a favorable correlation with UreC abundance [Citation176]. The enhancement of soil fertility can be achieved through appropriate factors such as biological activity, organic matter concentration, and sorption capacity. The biological index of soil fertility (F) is the function of three variables, these are (a) the frequency of bacteria group regrowth or soil enzyme activity (E), (b) soil organic matter content (M), (c) total sorption capacity of the soil (S).

The biological index of soil fertility (F)=f(E,M,S); F=√(E2+M2+S2)

The application of specific, efficient isolates of the ureolytic bacteria Bacillus sp. and Streptomyces sp. to barley and rice seedlings resulted in an increase in dry matter yields [Citation177]. This can be attributed to the provision of sufficient nitrogen from urea compounds to the plants. The urease that occurs naturally in soil is referred to as native urease, and it possesses remarkable stability. The components of the soil protect native urease from chemical degradation that results in the deactivation of enzymes as well as microbial degradation caused by proteases [Citation178]. The introduction purified urease into soil has temporarily increased the rate of urea hydrolysis, however readily deactivated or degraded by soil microorganisms as shown in . This suggests that the inherent stability of native urease surpasses that of externally introduced urease in soil. Additionally, the findings imply that the indigenous urease completely saturated the protective sites in soils, rendering them unable to protect the externally introduced urease. Harnessing the potential of ureolytic microbes represents a promising avenue for sustainable agriculture, offering opportunities to enhance nutrient cycling, improve soil fertility, and mitigate environmental degradation. Furthermore, the enhanced nutrient retention capacity conferred by microbial-mediated processes serves to mitigate nutrient runoff, thereby reducing the potential for eutrophication in adjacent aquatic ecosystems [Citation13].

Therapeutic potential of microbial ureases

The extensive body of literature on ureases underscores the profound interest of the scientific community in this enzyme, owing to its multifaceted versatility. Its utility extends beyond the agricultural and environmental sectors to encompass biomedical applications as well. Urease is pivotal in addressing bacterial infections associated with gastric ulcers, urinary tract infections, and kidney stones. Inhibiting urease activation by targeting its chaperone interactions, particularly with Ni(II) ions, offers a promising avenue for developing antimicrobial strategies against ureolytic pathogens like: Helicobacter pylori, Staphylococcus epidermidis, Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Proteus vulgaris [Citation36]. A diverse array of compounds, such as: thiophosphoryl triamides, aza-heterocyclics, dithiobisacetamides, and thiazole derivatives, has been reviewed as urease inhibitors, exhibiting varying IC50 values [Citation179]. Notably, dithiobisacetamide derivatives emerged with robust inhibitory potential against both cell-free urease and urease within intact cells, demonstrating negligible cytotoxicity to mammalian cells even at concentrations as high as 250 µM [Citation180]. Furthermore, the exploration of existing drugs like ropinirole and captopril as urease inhibitors has unveiled promising leads in the realm of drug repurposing, advancing the trajectory of urease inhibitor drug development [Citation126]. A pilot study suggests that bacteria may trigger rheumatoid arthritis (RA) through a mechanism involving molecular mimicry of urease to host proteins. The sequence/structure mimetics of flap fragments from bacterial ureases could potentially serve as peptidomimetics on a peptide chip in the future, enabling early prognosis of RA patients [Citation181].

Removal of urea from dialysate fluid

The human body primarily generates urea as its major nitrogenous waste, which can lead to the formation of potentially harmful byproducts such as guanidines and carbamylation products. Consequently, the elimination of urea becomes essential, particularly in conditions such as chronic kidney disease or renal failure [Citation182,Citation183]. Enzymatic urea hydrolysis has emerged as a promising approach for removing urea from dialysate, as illustrated in . However, several challenges hinder its widespread application, including the non-reusability and low storage stability of native urease, along with the heightened toxicity of ammonium, a byproduct of urea hydrolysis [Citation184]. To overcome these challenges, a urease-based urea removal strategy should be complemented by an ammonium elimination approach, such as the REcirculation DialYsis (REDY) sorbent system [Citation14]. Nevertheless, the use of materials such as zirconium phosphate/zirconium oxide or aluminum oxide as sorbents in this system complicates the process and increases costs, limiting its applicability in wearable artificial kidneys. Recent advancements, including microencapsulated multi-enzyme systems and innovative immobilization techniques, offer promising solutions to enhance the efficiency, stability, and practicality of urease-based systems in wearable artificial kidneys [Citation182]. For instance, Zhang et al. demonstrated successful urease immobilization onto carboxyl-functionalized polyether sulfone (PES) beads through covalent grafting, resulting in effective urea removal with a capacity of 75.1 mg/g after 480 min of incubation in a urea solution. Furthermore, the immobilized urease beads exhibited favorable thermal stability and blood compatibility, characterized by low protein adsorption, minimal clotting times, and a low hemolysis ratio. Notably, these beads retained their bioactivity for urea removal over multiple recycling cycles (up to five cycles) [Citation183].

Figure 6. Potential clinical and healthcare applications of microbial ureases.

Urease biosensors

Urease-based urea biosensors catalyze the hydrolysis of urea into NH4+ and HCO3− ions, increasing solution conductivity near the electrode. Guilbault and Montalvo pioneered the potentiometric urea electrode for urea determination [Citation125]. In addition to potentiometric biosensors, optical fiber biosensors have been developed by immobilizing urease in Immobilon membranes, detecting urea within a range of 0.1–10 mM alongside IgG. Another biosensor employs a urease-immobilized membrane for electrochemical signal generation in fluids containing urea, making it suitable for portable applications such as point-of-care testing and real-time monitoring [Citation185]. Furthermore, a stable potentiometric urea biosensor, utilizing poly(2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate-glycidyl methacrylate) nanoparticles as a carrier for urease, detects urea within a wide range of 0.01 mM to 500 mM, even in the presence of interfering substances, such as ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UR), glucose (GL), and sodium ions (Na+) [Citation186].

Anticancer activity

Urease activity initiates the catabolism of urea in the interstitial medium surrounding cancer cells, resulting in the production of metabolites like ammonia and hydroxide ions. These metabolic by-products are expected to induce stress on cancerous cells through both direct toxicity and alkaline induction. Studies have established the cytotoxicity of ammonia toward hepatocellular carcinoma cells, accompanied by an increase in pH in the adjacent medium due to urea breakdown [Citation187]. Urease exhibited targeted cytotoxicity toward A549 (Lung) and MDA-MB-231 (Breast) tumor cells, with LC50 values of 0.22 and 0.45 U/ml, respectively [Citation188,Citation189], attributed to the production of toxic levels of ammonia. The reversible urease inhibitor, acetohydroxamic acid, has been found effective in inhibiting these cytotoxic effects.

Removal of dental plaque

Ureolytic oral microflora, particularly S. salivarius and A. naeslundii, play a key role in urea metabolism, leading to the production of ammonia (NH3), which possesses anti-cariogenic effects [Citation63,Citation190], as illustrated in . This process has been observed to moderate plaque acidification and potentially prevent dental caries [Citation191]. The breakdown of urea by ureolytic microbes rapidly generates alkaline compounds, effectively neutralizing acidic substances like glycolytic acids in saliva and elevating pH levels, thereby neutralizing the oral environment. Consequently, caries-free individuals exhibit higher urease activity compared to caries-active groups [Citation192]. A clinical approach revealed that after an oral carbohydrate rinse and chewing urea-added gum, higher urease activity is generated, boosting the alkalogenic potential of saliva and plaque and thus preventing caries through an endogenic mechanism. Morou-Bermudez et al. also asserted a positive correlation between salivary urease activity and the development of dental cavities [Citation193], illustrating a statistically significant decrease in salivary urease levels in children who had ingested sugar-containing foods prior to sample collection compared to those who had fasted since the previous night. Site-specific urease activity measurements conducted by RUT study indicated significantly higher urease activity in interproximal supragingival plaque samples collected from the interproximal site of the lower central incisors (31/41) compared to samples from the interproximal sites of the upper central incisors (11/12), upper left first molar and premolar (26–25), and lower right first molar and premolar (45–46) [Citation63].

Current glitches and potential solutions

The microbial urease presents numerous opportunities, particularly in the realm of sustainable agriculture and environmental protection. The growing awareness and adoption of eco-friendly agricultural practices are driving the demand for urease-based fertilizers, which enhance soil fertility and crop yield. Additionally, the increasing focus on precision farming and controlled-release fertilizers is creating new opportunities for innovative urease formulations in synergy with urease inhibitors. Chemicals like: N-(n-butyl) thiophosphoric triamide (NBPT), N-(2-nitrophenyl) phosphoric triamide (2-NPT), N-(n-propyl) thiophosphoric triamide (NPPT), and phenyl phosphorodiamidate (PPD), decrease both soil NH4+ and NO3− availability and, thus, serve as effective tools to reduce nitrogen loss due to ammonia volatilization and NO3− leaching [Citation194–196]. Ongoing research in these areas is expected to further propel the demand for urease and its inhibitors.

Ureolysis-driven MICP has shown promising outcomes but faces certain challenges that may act as restrainers. These include thermal and pH instability [Citation197], rapid loss of activity [Citation115], and uncontrolled ammonia buildup [Citation5], leading to cytotoxicity and environmental imbalance. Microbial-induced struvite precipitation (MISP), an improved version of MICP, has recently gained interest due to its effectiveness in reducing ammonia-nitrogen pollution. This process involves introducing magnesium and phosphate ions, which react with ammonia to form magnesium ammonium phosphate (struvite) while releasing hydrogen ions [Citation198]. Furthermore, enriching urease-producing microbial communities in seawater has demonstrated MICP’s potential in mitigating desertification by inducing CaCO3 precipitation, which stabilizes loose sand and prevents soil erosion [Citation19]. However, field-scale applications of MICP for the bioremediation of heavy metal ions such as Hg2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+ can disrupt urease activity by binding to the active site and displacing essential Ni2+ ions, leading to enzyme inhibition [Citation199]. The growing prevalence of chelators such as ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA) can selectively bind heavy metals, preventing their interaction with urease [Citation200]. Additionally, advanced carriers and immobilization techniques, such as encapsulating microbes in polymer matrices or hydrogel beads, have helped protect microbial cells from environmental stress while maintaining sustained urease activity [Citation201]. The integration of genetically modified ureolytic bacteria with enhanced urease activity [Citation202] and comparative genomic and proteomic studies to identify novel accessory proteins across diverse microorganisms will be critical for overcoming these restrainers and facilitating the transition of microbial urease from laboratory research to full-scale industrial applications. Another primary challenge is the high cost of production and purification of microbial urease, especially for obtaining high-purity enzymes with specific activity required in clinical applications [Citation18,Citation20]. While the potential use of urease in developing diagnostic kits for detecting H. pylori infection is creating new opportunities in this field, the stringency of potential sources and the demand for highly purified products may restrain the widespread adoption of microbial urease.

Conclusions

Studies on urease date back to the 1870s, but only in the last 50 years has the ubiquitous nature of ureases beyond urea hydrolysis been explored through diverse emerging applications. Modern research highlights microbial ureases as versatile enzymes with broad substrate specificity and enantioselectivity across various conditions. Advances in agriculture, environmental science, and medicine have driven efforts to enhance urease efficiency using recombinant DNA technologies and molecular modeling. Machine learning and molecular docking now aid in predicting antiurease compounds and identifying antagonist binding sites. Additionally, ureolysis-driven MICP shows promise for eco-friendly applications, though scalability remains underexplored. Insights from this review pave the way for future developments in urease-induced carbonate precipitation (UICP), encouraging interdisciplinary research and innovative applications aligned with the United Nations’ SDGs, fostering global progress in biotechnology and environmental sustainability.

Author contributions

A. Ojha conceived the study and wrote the draft of the manuscript. D. Das and T.K. Bandyopadhyay critically reviewed the full manuscript content. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

Disclosure statement

No potential conflict of interest was reported by the authors.
